_**Agent (default)**_

To use `khoa00t1@gmail.com` as your Git identity, set your name and email. You can do this **globally** (all repos) or **locally** (this repo only).

## 1. Set name and email

**Global (recommended for your machine):**

```bash
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "khoa00t1@gmail.com"
```

**Only for this repo** (`khoakaka.com`):

```bash
cd /Users/khoale/dev/github/khoakaka.com
git config user.name "Your Name"
git config user.email "khoa00t1@gmail.com"
```

Replace `"Your Name"` with the name you want on commits (e.g. `Khoa Le`).

## 2. Verify

```bash
git config user.email
git config user.name
```

Or see where each value comes from:

```bash
git config --list --show-origin | grep user
```

## 3. GitHub authentication (separate from commit email)

Commit email and login are different:

| Purpose | What to configure |
|--------|-------------------|
| **Commit author** | `user.email` = `khoa00t1@gmail.com` |
| **Push/pull to GitHub** | GitHub account login + SSH key or credential helper |

If this Gmail is your GitHub account email, add it in GitHub under **Settings → Emails** and verify it so commits link to your profile.

**HTTPS:**

```bash
gh auth login
# or use Git credential manager when you push
```

**SSH (common):**

```bash
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "khoa00t1@gmail.com"
# Add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub to GitHub → Settings → SSH keys
```

## 4. Optional: keep work/personal separate

Use global config for personal work and per-repo config for work:

```bash
# In a work repo only:
git config user.email "work@company.com"
```

---

There’s no `user.name` or `user.email` set in this repo right now. If you want, I can run the commands for you — tell me the display name you want and whether to set it **globally** or **only for this repo**.

_**Agent (default)**_

Added `gem "rexml"` to your `Gemfile`:

```7:8:Gemfile
# Ruby 3.0+ no longer bundles rexml; Jekyll still requires it.
gem "rexml"
```

**Why this fixes it:** Ruby 3.0+ removed `rexml` from the default gems. Jekyll (and some of its dependencies) still `require` it, which causes:

```
cannot load such file -- rexml/parsers/baseparser (LoadError)
```

Your CI uses Ruby 3.3.11, so this is the expected fix.

**Next step:** Commit and push the `Gemfile` change. CI will run `bundle install` (via `bundler-cache: true`) and pick up `rexml` automatically. `Gemfile.lock` is gitignored here, so you don't need to commit a lockfile.

I couldn't run `bundle install` locally because this machine has Ruby 2.6, while CI uses 3.3 — that's fine; the workflow will resolve it on push.

The likely fixes are downgrading Ruby to 3.2 in CI, upgrading to a modern Jekyll version, or correcting the configuration.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Jekyll 3.6.2 fails with a "no implicit conversion of Hash into Integer" error when reading files under Ruby 3.3. This is a known compatibility issue.

The root cause appears to be Jekyll 3.6.2 from the github-pages gem being extremely outdated. It is incompatible with Ruby 3.3. The CI workflow specifies ruby-version: "3.3", which likely conflicts with GitHub Pages' current Ruby version.

Three fixes are being considered: downgrading CI to Ruby 3.2 or 3.1, dropping the github-pages gem for a modern Jekyll install, or correcting the _config.yml structure. The error may stem from malformed defaults or timezone settings in older Jekyll versions.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
CI appears to be resolving Jekyll 3.6.

GitHub Pages uses Jekyll 3.10.0 with github-pages 232. Without a Gemfile.lock, Bundler may be pulling an old version from stale cache. Duplicate plugin declarations in the Gemfile could also be causing dependency resolution issues.

Explicit gems inside the `:jekyll_plugins` group likely conflict with `github-pages` 232 and force an older Jekyll version. The Gemfile should be modernized to use `~> 232` or allow automatic resolution.

Since the site deploys through GitHub Actions, Jekyll 4.x can be used directly instead of being forced onto the github-pages gem. The Gemfile should drop duplicate plugin gems because github-pages already includes them. Ruby 3.x standard library gems such as base64 and bigdecimal need explicit entries. The workflow cache-version should be bumped to clear stale Bundler data. Committing Gemfile.lock would ensure reproducible builds.

GitHub Actions deployments only require the generated `_site` output, so the `github-pages` gem is unnecessary. The Gemfile should declare Jekyll 3.10 directly alongside its required plugins and standard library dependencies. This matches the GitHub Pages runtime environment.

Gemfile.lock is gitignored, so Bundler normally resolves fresh dependencies each run. However, the workflow cache may still restore an old lockfile from a previous build, which would explain why Jekyll 3.6.2 persists despite the Gemfile changes.

GitHub recommends pinning `github-pages` to `~> 232` and adding `rexml` and `webrick` explicitly. The empty `:jekyll_plugins` group can be removed entirely. For Actions-based deployments, the standard pattern is to declare `gem "jekyll", "~> 4.3"` directly rather than using the `github-pages` gem.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The local Ruby version is 4.0.2. The github-pages gem does not yet support Ruby 4.0.

CI uses Ruby 3.3, so Gemfile changes should work there. The build failure stems from a stale Jekyll 3.6.2 cache. Pinning github-pages '~> 232' and bumping the cache version should resolve it. Another option is switching to direct Jekyll gems instead of github-pages, which would bypass the gem constraints entirely.

A direct Jekyll setup pins Jekyll 3.10 alongside required gems like rexml, webrick, base64, bigdecimal, and csv. This removes github-pages entirely and aligns with GitHub Pages' official Ruby 3.3 and Jekyll 3.10 stack.

Dropping github-pages would also remove bundled meta-gems like jekyll-theme-primer and jekyll-titles-from-headings. The titles plugin crashed because document.content was nil after earlier read failures. Fixing the pathutil conflict with github-pages 232 should resolve the build.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The Jekyll build failed with exit code 1. Warnings indicate base64 and bigdecimal will be removed from default gems in Ruby 3.4.0. Bundler threw an error during execution.

The `github-pages` meta-gem was replaced with explicit `jekyll ~> 3.10` and plugins. Standard library gems including `base64`, `bigdecimal`, and `csv` were added to the Gemfile. The CI cache version was bumped and `Gemfile.lock` was generated and committed. These changes should be pushed. I will not commit automatically. I am also verifying whether Jekyll 3.10 will fail on `pathutil` under Ruby 3.3.

The build failure likely stems from a `pathutil` keyword argument bug in Jekyll 3.6.2 that breaks file reading during generation. Jekyll 3.9.2 removed its main dependency on `pathutil`. I am checking whether Jekyll 3.10 still pulls in a vulnerable version.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Here's what was going wrong and what changed.

## Root cause

CI was running **Jekyll 3.6.2** (from an old `github-pages` resolve / stale Bundler cache) on **Ruby 3.3**. That combo breaks with:

```
no implicit conversion of Hash into Integer
```

GitHub Pages currently uses **Jekyll 3.10.0** on Ruby 3.3 — your CI was several major versions behind.

## Fixes applied

1. **`Gemfile`** — Replaced the `github-pages` meta-gem with explicit gems:
   - `jekyll ~> 3.10` (matches [GitHub Pages versions](https://pages.github.com/versions/))
   - Your four plugins directly (`jekyll-seo-tag`, `jekyll-sitemap`, `jekyll-feed`, `jekyll-remote-theme`)
   - Stdlib gems Ruby no longer bundles: `rexml`, `webrick`, `base64`, `bigdecimal`, `csv`

2. **`Gemfile.lock`** — Generated with Jekyll **3.10.0** locked, and removed from `.gitignore` so CI gets reproducible installs.

3. **`.github/workflows/jekyll.yml`** — Bumped `cache-version` from `0` to `1` to clear the stale `vendor/bundle` cache that was still serving Jekyll 3.6.2.

## Next step

Commit and push:

- `Gemfile`
- `Gemfile.lock` (new)
- `.gitignore`
- `.github/workflows/jekyll.yml`

The Ruby 3.4 warnings (`base64`, `bigdecimal`, `csv`) should also be gone now that those gems are explicit. The `jekyll-titles-from-headings` crash was a follow-on failure after files failed to read — it should disappear once Jekyll 3.10 builds cleanly.

---

Jekyll 3.9+ needs this gem for GitHub Flavored Markdown. The old `github-pages` meta-gem pulled it in automatically; with direct `jekyll` dependencies it has to be declared explicitly.
